sources.notes: Interactive statement for the hard version with the larger hidden range.
Official editorial confirms scaling/block constants and the candidate-encoding second query.

annotation.summary: Recover the hidden width in the hard interactive version with a bounded number of queries.
annotation.constraints: The hidden range is larger than D1, so the candidate set must be compressed by a carefully scaled first query before encoding.
annotation.core_idea: Use a long structured first query whose answer localizes the hidden value to a short interval or block. Then construct a second query that assigns distinguishable responses to all candidates in that interval. Constants are chosen so all encoded values remain within the interactive limits.
annotation.complexity: O(1) queries and O(K) local candidate encoding
tricks: Scale the first query to reduce a large range to a short candidate interval.
Encode the remaining candidates in a second query.
Tune constants so the protocol stays within answer bounds.

solution_variants.name: scaled-two-query-encoding
solution_variants.summary: Use a block-scaled first probe to localize the answer, then distinguish remaining candidates by a crafted second probe.

tags.evidence: The solution is a fixed query protocol for reconstructing the hidden width from oracle responses.
The hard part is making a second query whose response uniquely identifies each remaining candidate.
tags.description: Design query values so that oracle responses encode enough information to distinguish a finite candidate set.
tags.created_reason: This interactive encoding pattern is reusable and more specific than a generic interactive reconstruction tag.

tags.evidence: After localization, the implementation enumerates the short candidate set.
Candidate localization uses quotient/divisibility properties of the response.
The algorithm explicitly constructs both query arrays to force distinguishable responses.
